42: A fulfillment: the stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. Jesus applies Psalms 118:22-23 to himself: he is the stone rejected by Israel's builders (the chief priests and elders, Matthew 21:23) that God has made the cornerstone of the new temple — the community of those who receive the kingdom (Matthew 21:43). The stone-metaphor has a long OT trajectory (Isaiah 28:16, Zechariah 3:9) culminating in this: the rejected one is the foundational stone without which the entire structure of God's redemptive purposes would collapse. The crucifixion-and-resurrection is the act of rejection-and-vindication that fulfills the Psalm.
1: A direct fulfillment: "Now when they drew near to Jerusalem and came to Bethphage, to the Mount of Olives, then Jesus sent two disciples." The approach to Jerusalem via Bethphage on the Mount of Olives is the specific geographical choreography of the royal entry. The Mount of Olives is the site where Zechariah 14:4 places YHWH's feet at the eschatological day of the LORD. Jesus descends from the exact site that the prophet associated with divine arrival. The Christological geography: the route is not incidental but prophetically loaded.
2: A direct fulfillment: "Saying to them, 'Go into the village in front of you, and immediately you will find a donkey tied, and a colt with her. Untie them and bring them to me.'" The prior knowledge of the tied donkey and colt — either by divine foreknowledge or pre-arrangement — demonstrates Jesus' sovereign orchestration of his own prophetic fulfillment. The Christological precision: the details of the entry are under his control, not incidental. He enters Jerusalem as the one who scripts his own fulfillment of prophecy.
3: A revelation of God: "If anyone says anything to you, you shall say, 'The Lord needs them,' and he will send them at once." The "the Lord needs them" (ho kyrios autōn chreian echei) is both practical instruction and Christological self-identification. The Lord, who owns everything (Psalms 24:1), declares need — the incarnate Son who has genuinely entered the condition of human dependency. The submission of the donkey's owner to this request is the submission of creation to its rightful Lord.
4: A direct fulfillment: "This took place to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet, saying..." Matthew's fulfillment formula marks the entry as the conscious realization of prophetic Scripture. The Christological hermeneutic: the entry into Jerusalem is not a royal display that happens to resemble Zechariah; it is the deliberate enactment of the prophetic script. Jesus enters Jerusalem as the one who knows he is fulfilling the word of God.
5: A direct fulfillment: "'Say to the daughter of Zion, 'Behold, your king is coming to you, humble, and mounted on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a beast of burden.''" The citation of Zechariah 9:9 (combined with Isaiah 62:11) specifies the kind of kingship on display: not the war-horse of military conquest but the donkey of humble peace. The Christological kingship is a deliberate anti-type of Davidic warrior-kingship. The one who comes is the king of peace whose dominion will extend to the ends of the earth (Zechariah 9:10) — but by way of the cross, not the sword.
6: A revelation of God: "The disciples went and did as Jesus had directed them." The disciples' obedience to the precise instructions is the Christological partnership: they execute the prophetic script that Jesus directs. The entry into Jerusalem is not a spontaneous popular event but a carefully choreographed fulfillment of Scripture, with Jesus as both the subject of the prophecy and the director of its enactment.
7: A revelation of God: "They brought the donkey and the colt and put on them their cloaks, and he sat on them." The spreading of cloaks on the animals is the disciples' act of honor — preparing a royal seat for the king. The Christological symbolism: the humble king rides on borrowed animals, seated on disciples' cloaks, entering the city where he will be crucified within the week. The royalty and the humility are inseparable.
8: A direct fulfillment: "Most of the crowd spread their cloaks on the road, and others cut branches from the trees and spread them on the road." The spreading of cloaks on the road echoes 2 Kings 9:13, where garments were spread before Jehu when he was proclaimed king. The palm branches echo the Feast of Tabernacles (Leviticus 23:40) and the Maccabean victory celebration (1 Macc Matthew 13:51). The crowd is acting out a coronation — the Christological import: they recognize something kingly, though they will misunderstand what kind of king has arrived.
9: A direct fulfillment: "And the crowds that went before him and that followed him were shouting, 'Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest!'" The Hosanna-shout is a citation of Psalms 118:25-26 (hōshi'a nā, "save us, we pray"). The Christological identification: "Son of David" from the crowd affirms the messianic lineage; "he who comes in the name of the LORD" is the one sent from God. The Hosanna is both an exclamation of praise and a petition for salvation — "save us!" directed to the one who will do exactly that, though not in the way the crowd expects.
10: A revelation of God: "And when he entered Jerusalem, the whole city was stirred up, saying, 'Who is this?'" The stirring of the whole city (eseisthē pasa hē polis — the same verb for earthquake, seismos) is the Christological disruption that Jesus' arrival always creates. The question "who is this?" echoes Matthew 12:23 ("Can this be the Son of David?") and is the question every person must answer. The whole city shaken by his arrival anticipates the earthquake at the resurrection (Matthew 28:2) — the same presence that disrupts the earth.
11: A revelation of God: "And the crowds said, 'This is the prophet Jesus, from Nazareth of Galilee.'" The crowd's identification — "the prophet Jesus, from Nazareth of Galilee" — is the prophetic category, not the full messianic confession. They see more than an ordinary teacher but less than the Son of God. The Christological inadequacy of the crowd's answer echoes Matthew 16:14 (the popular identifications before Caesarea Philippi). Jesus is more than a prophet; the crowd's answer is the same category-error repeated at the triumphal entry.
12: A direct revelation: "And Jesus entered the temple and drove out all who sold and bought in the temple, and he overturned the tables of the money-changers and the seats of those who sold pigeons." The Temple Cleansing is the Christological authority-act that makes the messianic claim most explicit. Jesus enters the Temple — his Father's house (Matthew 21:13) — and acts with the authority of its owner. The driving out is the act of the one who has standing to judge the Temple's use. Only the Son could do what the Son does here.
13: A direct fulfillment: "He said to them, 'It is written, My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you make it a den of robbers.'" The "my house" (ho oikos mou) is the Christological self-identification as the Son: the Temple belongs to his Father, and he speaks of it with filial ownership. The citation of Isaiah 56:7 (house of prayer for all peoples) paired with Jeremiah 7:11 (den of robbers) is the prophetic indictment applied to the present situation. Jesus does not merely quote the prophets; he fulfills the prophetic office of calling Israel to account.
14: A revelation of God: "And the blind and the lame came to him in the temple, and he healed them." The healing of the blind and lame in the Temple — immediately after the cleansing — is the Christological replacement of the commercial with the compassionate. The Temple becomes what it should be: the place of divine encounter and healing, not commercial transaction. Note: 2 Samuel 5:8 associates the blind and lame with exclusion from the Temple; Jesus receives and heals the very ones previously excluded.
15: A revelation of God: "But when the chief priests and the scribes saw the wonderful things that he did, and the children crying out in the temple, 'Hosanna to the Son of David!' they were indignant." The chief priests' indignation at the children's Hosanna is the Christological contrast: the religious establishment is outraged by the messianic worship that children offer freely. The children in the Temple crying "Son of David" complete the triumphal entry scene — the kingdom belongs to children (Matthew 19:14), and the children recognize the king.
16: A direct fulfillment: "And they said to him, 'Do you hear what these are saying?' And Jesus said to them, 'Yes; have you never read, 'Out of the mouth of infants and nursing babies you have prepared praise?''" The citation of Psalms 8:2 — where the praise of infants silences the enemy — is the Christological counter to the chief priests' objection. The children's worship is not an embarrassment but a fulfillment of Scripture: God has prepared praise from the mouths of the very young. The one who receives the children's worship is the one to whom the Psalm applies.
17: A revelation of God: "And leaving them, he went out of the city to Bethany and lodged there." The withdrawal to Bethany — the village of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus — is the Christological pattern of withdrawal after confrontation: the Temple episode and its aftermath require a strategic retreat. The lodging in Bethany (2 miles from Jerusalem) is also the base for the final week's daily journey to and from Jerusalem. The one who has no permanent home (Matthew 8:20) is hosted by friends on the way to his death.
18: A type: "In the morning, as he was returning to the city, he became hungry." The hunger of Jesus on the way back to Jerusalem is the genuine human vulnerability of the incarnate Son. The Christological resonance: the one who fed five thousand is himself hungry. The bodily need that will be the occasion for the fig tree's cursing is not staged but real. The Son of God who made himself nothing (Philippians 2:7) experiences ordinary human hunger as he walks toward the passion.
19: A type: "And seeing a fig tree by the wayside, he went to it and found nothing on it but only leaves. And he said to it, 'May no fruit ever come from you again!' And the fig tree withered at once." The cursing of the fig tree is the enacted parable of Israel's religious profession without fruit — leaves (external observance) without figs (genuine righteousness). The fig tree that promises fruit but produces none is the image of temple religion that has been cleansed (Matthew 21:12-13) but will not produce the fruit of repentance (Matthew 3:10). The immediate withering is the Christological judgment pronounced with divine authority.
20: A revelation of God: "When the disciples saw it, they marveled, saying, 'How did the fig tree wither at once?'" The disciples' astonishment at the immediate withering focuses on the mechanism — "how?" — before Jesus redirects to the principle: faith in God. The Christological bearing: the miracle of the withered fig tree is not merely a display of power but a lesson in the relationship between faith and the authority the disciples have been given. The withering is the visible sign of the invisible verdict.
21: A direct revelation: "And Jesus answered them, 'Truly, I say to you, if you have faith and do not doubt, you will not only do what has been done to the fig tree, but even if you say to this mountain, 'Be taken up and thrown into the sea,' it will happen.'" The mountain-moving faith (already promised in Matthew 17:20) is the Christological extension of the fig-tree miracle: the disciples who receive Christ's faith can act with his authority. The mountain cast into the sea is the eschatological imagery of Zechariah 14:4 — the Mount of Olives split at the day of the LORD. Kingdom authority can accomplish what seems geologically impossible.
22: A revelation of God: "And whatever you ask in prayer, you will receive, if you have faith." The prayer-promise connected to the fig-tree episode grounds answered prayer in faith. The Christological basis: the disciples pray in Jesus' name (cf. John 14:13-14) — the authority of the name is the ground of confidence. Faith that asks is faith directed to the one who has all authority, and that authority is not withheld from those who ask in dependence on him.
23: A direct revelation: "And when he entered the temple, the chief priests and the elders of the people came up to him as he was teaching, and said, 'By what authority are you doing these things, and who gave you this authority?'" The authority question — "by what authority?" — is the central Christological question of the passion week. The chief priests and elders challenge the basis of the Temple cleansing and the teaching. The Christological irony: the one who has all authority (Matthew 28:18) is being interrogated about the source of his authority by the institution that should have recognized him.
24: A direct revelation: "Jesus answered them, 'I also will ask you one question, and if you tell me the answer, then I also will tell you by what authority I do these things.'" The counter-question is the Christological epistemological move: to answer the authority-question directly would require them to either accept or explicitly reject his divine authority. The counter-question about John's baptism creates a dilemma that exposes their real position.
25: A direct revelation: "The baptism of John, from where did it come? From heaven or from man?' And they discussed it among themselves, saying, 'If we say, From heaven, he will say to us, Why then did you not believe him?'" The dilemma Jesus creates is the Christological trap: John's authority and Jesus' authority are from the same source — and rejecting one requires rejecting the other. The chief priests' calculation reveals they are not interested in truth but in political safety. The one who poses the dilemma knows their hearts.
26: A revelation of God: "But if we say, From man, we are afraid of the crowd, for they all hold that John was a prophet." The fear of the crowd reveals that the chief priests' rejection of John and Jesus is political rather than theological. The Christological exposure: those who should be theological authorities operate by crowd-management, not prophetic discernment. The authority they exercise is human not divine — the very charge they are bringing against Jesus turns back on them.
27: A direct revelation: "So they answered Jesus, 'We do not know.' And he said to them, 'Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.'" The refusal to answer ("we do not know") forfeits their claim to judge Jesus' authority. The Christological verdict: those who will not acknowledge the Father's authority through John are unqualified to judge the Father's authority through the Son. The non-answer to the counter-question is itself an answer to the original question — by the authority the rejectors cannot name.
28: A revelation of God: "What do you think? A man had two sons. And he went to the first and said, 'Son, go and work in the vineyard today.'" The Two Sons parable opens with a father-and-sons scenario that mirrors the Father-Son relationship throughout Matthew. The Christological setting: Jesus is speaking to the chief priests and elders who have just refused to answer his question. The parable will identify them with the son who says yes but does nothing.
29: A revelation of God: "And he answered, 'I will not,' but afterward he changed his mind and went." The first son's refusal followed by repentance and obedience is the Christological portrait of the tax collectors and sinners who initially refused the kingdom's call but responded to John's preaching (Matthew 21:32). The change of mind (metamelomai) is not the full metanoia of repentance but the practical turn to obedience. Action counts more than initial profession.
30: A revelation of God: "And he went to the other and said the same. And he answered, 'I go, sir,' but did not go." The second son's profession without action is the Christological portrait of the chief priests who say "I go, Lord" with their mouths while their hearts and actions remain far from the Father. The religious establishment that professes to do the Father's will has not entered the kingdom. Verbal compliance without action is the anti-type of true discipleship.
31: A direct revelation: "Which of the two did the will of his father?' They said, 'The first.' Jesus said to them, 'Truly, I say to you, the tax collectors and the prostitutes go into the kingdom of God before you.'" The verdict — tax collectors and prostitutes enter the kingdom before the chief priests — is the Christological inversion of religious expectation. The religiously despised (those who initially said "I will not") have entered through repentance; the religiously respectable (those who said "I go" but did not) have not. Kingdom entry is by changed life, not by position.
32: A direct revelation: "For John came to you in the way of righteousness, and you did not believe him, but the tax collectors and the prostitutes believed him. And even when you saw it, you did not afterward change your minds and believe him." The Christological indictment: the chief priests saw the effect of John's preaching on tax collectors and prostitutes — the first sons going to work in the vineyard — but did not change. The evidence of genuine repentance was visible to them and still did not move them. Hardness of heart is willful, not ignorant.
33: A direct revelation: "Hear another parable. There was a master of a house who planted a vineyard and put a fence around it and dug a winepress in it and built a tower and leased it to tenants, and went into a far country." The Wicked Tenants parable is an allegory drawn directly from Isaiah 5:1-7 (the Song of the Vineyard, where the vineyard is Israel). The householder who plants, fences, digs, builds, and leases is the Father who prepared Israel for the kingdom's harvest. The Christological setup: the master goes away, and the tenants' management of the vineyard will reveal their character.
34: A revelation of God: "When the season for fruit drew near, he sent his servants to the tenants to get his fruit." The servants sent to collect the harvest are the prophets sent to Israel to call for the fruit of repentance and righteousness. The Christological frame: the entire prophetic tradition — from Moses to John — is the master's repeated sending of servants to collect what the vineyard owes.
35: A revelation of God: "And the tenants took his servants and beat one, killed another, and stoned another." The violence against the servants is the history of Israel's rejection of the prophets: beaten (Jeremiah in the stocks, Jeremiah 20:2), killed (Uriah, Jeremiah 26:23), stoned (Zechariah, 2 Chronicles 24:21). The Christological catalog: the servants' fate is the fate of every prophet sent to call Israel to account. The pattern of rejection is not new — it is the recurring tragedy of the vineyard.
36: A revelation of God: "Again he sent other servants, more than the first. And they did the same to them." The repeated sending — more servants, the same rejection — is the divine patience with Israel across the centuries. The Christological bearing: the master does not give up after the first rejection. The multiplication of servants (prophets) before sending the son is the measure of the master's longsuffering. The final servant is not the first response but the culmination of a long history of attempts.
37: A direct revelation: "Finally he sent his son to them, saying, 'They will respect my son.'" The sending of the son after all the servants have been rejected is the Christological identification of the parable's central point: Jesus is the Son sent last, after all the prophets. The master's expectation — "they will respect my son" — is the divine logic of incarnation: the Son, as the final and definitive word (Hebrews 1:1-2), will be recognized as the one the servants could not be. The expectation is tragically wrong — not the Father's miscalculation but the measure of the tenants' wickedness.
38: A direct revelation: "But when the tenants saw the son, they said to themselves, 'This is the heir. Come, let us kill him and have his inheritance.'" The recognition of the son — "this is the heir" — and the deliberate decision to kill him is the Christological indictment of the chief priests and Sanhedrin: they know who he is (or suspect it), and they choose murder over submission. The plan to seize the inheritance by killing the heir is the logic of those who want the kingdom without the king.
39: A direct revelation: "And they took him and threw him out of the vineyard and killed him." The killing of the son — thrown out of the vineyard before being killed — has the Christological detail that mirrors the passion: Jesus is led outside the city to be crucified (Matthew 27:32; cf. Hebrews 13:12: "Jesus suffered outside the gate"). The parable predicts the spatial pattern of the passion: expelled from the city, then killed. The allegory is precise in its prophecy.
40: A direct revelation: "When therefore the owner of the vineyard comes, what will he do to those tenants?" The question Jesus poses to the chief priests will produce their own judgment on themselves. The Christological irony: the chief priests are about to pronounce their own eschatological sentence.
41: A direct revelation: "They said to him, 'He will put those wretches to a miserable death and let out the vineyard to other tenants who will give him the fruits in their seasons.'" The chief priests' own verdict — the wretches destroyed, the vineyard given to new tenants — is the Christological self-judgment. Without knowing it, they have announced the eschatological consequence of rejecting the Son: the vineyard will be taken and given to others (Matthew 21:43). The judgment they pronounce is accurate and will be applied to them.
43: A direct revelation: "Therefore I tell you, the kingdom of God will be taken away from you and given to a people producing its fruits." The direct application of the parable to the chief priests is the Christological verdict: the vineyard (the kingdom of God) will be given to a people who produce fruit — the community of Jew and Gentile gathered around the crucified and risen Son. The Christological transfer: the nation-as-exclusive-vessel of the kingdom is superseded by the community constituted by faith in the Son.
44: A direct revelation: "And the one who falls on this stone will be broken to pieces; and when it falls on anyone, it will crush him." The stone of stumbling — Jesus as the cornerstone — causes two different forms of judgment: those who fall on it are broken; those on whom it falls are crushed. The Christological severity: rejection of the cornerstone is not merely religious failure but eschatological catastrophe. The same stone that is the foundation of the new temple (Matthew 21:42) is the stone of judgment for those who refuse it (Isaiah 8:14-15; Daniel 2:34-35, 44).
45: A revelation of God: "When the chief priests and the Pharisees heard his parables, they perceived that he was speaking about them." The recognition of self-incrimination — "he was speaking about them" — is the Christological moment of dawning clarity. The parables have been transparent enough that those with ears have heard. The chief priests and Pharisees now know exactly what Jesus thinks of their position, and they will respond not with repentance but with plotting.
46: A revelation of God: "And although they were seeking to arrest him, they feared the crowds, because they held him to be a prophet." The same political calculation as in Matthew 21:26 (fearing the crowd about John) now applies to Jesus. The Christological protection: the popular recognition of Jesus as a prophet prevents the arrest the Sanhedrin wants. The crowd's limited identification (prophet, not Messiah) is still sufficient to protect him — temporarily. The chief priests will find their opening in Judas (Matthew 26:14-16), who avoids the crowds by enabling a nighttime arrest.
